revision.text:
[[ar:ماساكو إمبراطورة اليابان]][[arz:ماساكو امبراطورة اليابان]][[ast:Masako Owada]][[azb:ماساکو]][[ca:Emperadriu Masako]][[cs:Masako]][[da:Masako af Japan]][[de:Masako (Kaiserin)]][[el:Αυτοκράτειρα Μασάκο της Ιαπωνίας]][[en:Empress Masako]][[eo:Masako]][[es:Masako]][[et:Masako]][[eu:Masako enperatriza]][[fa:ماساکو]][[fi:Masako (keisarinna)]][[fr:Masako Owada]][[he:מסאקו, קיסרית יפן]][[hu:Maszako japán császárné]][[hy:Կայսրուհի Մասակո]][[id:Permaisuri Masako]][[it:Imperatrice Masako]][[ja:皇后雅子]][[ka:მასაკო ოვადა]][[knc:Empress Masako]][[ko:마사코 황후]][[la:Imperatrix Masako]][[min:Owada Masako]][[mn:Овада Масако]][[ms:Maharani Masako]][[nl:Masako Owada]][[no:Masako av Japan]][[pl:Masako]][[pt:Masako do Japão]][[ro:Împărăteasa Masako]][[ru:Масако]][[simple:Empress Masako]][[skr:ماساکو]][[sv:Masako]][[th:สมเด็จพระจักรพรรดินีมาซาโกะ]][[tr:Masako (imparatoriçe)]][[uk:Імператриця Масако]][[ur:ماساکو]][[uz:Imperatoritsa Masako]][[vi:Hoàng hậu Masako]][[wuu:雅子皇后]][[zh-yue:雅子]][[zh:雅子皇后]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Empress Masako at TICAD7.jpg|birth_date={{birth date|1963|12|9}}|birth_place={{ill|Toranomon Hospital|qid=Q11625273|1=Toranomon Hospital}}|gender={{ill|female|qid=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|aristocrat|qid=Q2478141|1=Aristocracy (class)}}; {{ill|consort|qid=Q5784340|1=consort}}; {{ill|diplomat|qid=Q193391|1=Diplomat}}|title={{ill|Crown Princess of Japan|qid=Q986305|1=Crown Princess of Japan}}; {{ill|Empress of Japan|qid=Q595038|1=Empress of Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|1=Naruhito}}|children={{ill|Aiko, Princess Toshi|qid=Q743509|1=Aiko, Princess Toshi}}|father={{ill|Hisashi Owada|qid=Q701990|1=Hisashi Owada}}|mother={{ill|Yumiko Owada|qid=Q15059261|1=Yumiko Owada}}|relatives={{ill|Reiko Ikeda|qid=Q11552076|1=Reiko Ikeda}}; {{ill|Setsuko Shibuya|qid=Q55528547|1=Setsuko Shibuya}}|family={{ill|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}; {{ill|Owada family|qid=Q24874353|1=Owada family}}|awards={{ill|Dame Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic|qid=Q109729529|1=Dame Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic}}; {{ill|Grand Commander of the Order of the Defender of the Realm|qid=Q7434980|1=Grand Commander of the Order of the Defender of the Realm}}; {{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of Leopold|qid=Q14900501|1=Grand Cordon of the Order of Leopold}}; {{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Precious Crown|qid=Q64735577|1=Grand Cordon of the Order of the Precious Crown}}; {{ill|Grand Cross of Honor for Services to the Republic of Austria|qid=Q19883682|1=Grand Cross of Honor for Services to the Republic of Austria}}; {{ill|Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic|qid=Q17365974|1=Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic}}; {{ill|Grand Cross of the Order of Merit of Hungary|qid=Q458894|1=Grand Cross of the Order of Merit of Hungary}}; {{ill|Grand Cross of the Order of Prince Henry|qid=Q20859490|1=Grand Cross of the Order of Prince Henry}}; {{ill|Grand Cross of the Order of Rio Branco|qid=Q28865094|1=Grand Cross of the Order of Rio Branco}}; {{ill|Grand Cross of the Order of the Redeemer|qid=Q21450802|1=Grand Cross of the Order of the Redeemer}}; {{ill|King Willem-Alexander Inauguration Medal|qid=Q12215488|1=King Willem-Alexander Inauguration Medal}}; {{ill|Order of St. Olav|qid=Q94345|1=Order of St. Olav}}; {{ill|Order of Sālote Tupou III|qid=Q3885356|1=Order of Queen Sālote Tupou III}}; {{ill|Order of the Crown (Netherlands)|qid=Q2272104|1=Order of the Crown (Netherlands)}}; {{ill|Order of the Gold Lion of the House of Nassau|qid=Q93976|1=Order of the Gold Lion of the House of Nassau}}|religion={{ill|Shinto|qid=Q812767|1=Shinto}}|native_name=皇后雅子|given_name={{ill|Masako|qid=Q1906740|1=Masako}}}} {{short description|Empress of Japan since 2019}} {{use dmy dates|date=April 2024}} {{expand Japanese|topic=bio|date=June 2021}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Masako {{nobold | {{Ubl | {{native name|ja|雅子|italics=no|paren=omit}} }}}} | consort = yes | image = Naruhito and Masako visit Bogor Palace 48 (cropped) (2).jpg | image_size = 220px | caption = Masako in 2023 | succession = {{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|qid=Q595038|lt=Empress consort of Japan|1=Empress of Japan}} | reign = 1 May 2019 – present | coronation = 22 October 2019 | cor-type = [[2019 Japanese imperial transition#Enthronement Ceremony|Enthronement]] | spouse = {{marriage|{{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}}|9 June 1993}} | issue = {{ill|Aiko, Princess Toshi|en|Aiko, Princess Toshi|qid=Q743509|lt=Aiko, Princess Toshi|1=Aiko, Princess Toshi}} | royal house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} | father = {{ill|Hisashi Owada|en|Hisashi Owada|qid=Q701990|lt=Hisashi Owada|1=Hisashi Owada}} | education = {{ill|Harvard University|en|Harvard University|qid=Q13371|lt=Harvard University|1=Harvard University}} | mother = Yumiko Egashira | birth_name = Masako Owada
({{lang|ja|小和田雅子}}) | birth_date = {{Birth date and age|1963|12|9|df=y}} | birth_place = Toranomon Hospital, {{ill|Toranomon|en|Toranomon|qid=Q7825412|lt=Toranomon|1=Toranomon}}, {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|qid=Q190088|lt=Minato|1=Minato, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, Japan | death_date = | death_place = | burial_date = | burial_place = | signature = [[File:Empress Masako signature.svg|50px]] }} {{Japanese Imperial Family}} {{Nihongo|'''Masako'''|雅子||born {{nihongo|'''Masako Owada'''|小和田雅子|''Owada Masako''}}; 9 December 1963}} is {{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|qid=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan|1=Empress of Japan}} as the wife of {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}}. Born in Tokyo, Masako was educated at {{ill|Belmont High School (Massachusetts)|en|Belmont High School (Massachusetts)|qid=Q14715400|lt=Belmont High School|1=Belmont High School (Massachusetts)}} in Massachusetts before attending {{ill|Harvard College|en|Harvard College|qid=Q49123|lt=Harvard College|1=Harvard College}}, earning a {{ill|Bachelor of Arts|en|Bachelor of Arts|qid=Q1765120|lt=B.A.|1=Bachelor of Arts}}, ''[[:en:magna cum laude|magna cum laude]]'', in {{ill|Economics|en|Economics|qid=Q8134|lt=Economics|1=Economics}}.{{Cite news|date=2019-05-01|title=Empress Masako: The Japanese princess who struggles with royal life|language=en-GB|work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-48118128|access-date=2021-01-24}} She also studied law at the {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=University of Tokyo|1=University of Tokyo}} and {{ill|International relations|en|International relations|qid=Q166542|lt=International relations|1=International relations}} at {{ill|Balliol College, Oxford|en|Balliol College, Oxford|qid=Q805285|lt=Balliol College, Oxford|1=Balliol College, Oxford}}. She then worked for Japan's {{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q222241|lt=Ministry of Foreign Affairs|1=Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)}} as a {{ill|Diplomat|en|Diplomat|qid=Q193391|lt=Diplomat|1=Diplomat}}. Masako met Crown Prince Naruhito in 1986. They married in 1993; their only child, {{ill|Aiko, Princess Toshi|en|Aiko, Princess Toshi|qid=Q743509|lt=Aiko, Princess Toshi|1=Aiko, Princess Toshi}}, was born in 2001. The birth fueled the ongoing {{ill|Japanese imperial succession debate|en|Japanese imperial succession debate|qid=Q2996090|lt=Japanese imperial succession debate|1=Japanese imperial succession debate}}, which had resulted in some politicians holding a favorable view on rescinding [[:en:Agnatic primogeniture|Agnatic primogeniture]] imposed by {{ill|Allies of World War II|en|Allies of World War II|qid=Q329888|lt=World War II allies|1=Allies of World War II}} on the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}}. However, with the birth of {{ill|Prince Hisahito of Akishino|en|Prince Hisahito of Akishino|qid=Q844333|lt=a son|1=Prince Hisahito of Akishino}} to Naruhito's brother, {{ill|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|en|Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan|qid=Q311174|lt=Prince Fumihito|1=Fumihito, Crown Prince of Japan}}, in 2006, no amendments were made and Aiko remains ineligible to inherit the throne, so she cannot succeed her father.{{cite web |title=Japan Crown Prince Fumihito formally declared 1st in line to throne |url=https://english.kyodonews.net/news/2020/11/620836334ca3-update1-japan-crown-prince-formally-declared-1st-in-line-to-throne.html |website=english.kyodonews.net |publisher=Kyodo News |access-date=22 January 2023}} The pressure to produce a male heir affected Masako's health. She was diagnosed with {{ill|adjustment disorder|en|adjustment disorder|qid=Q317158|lt=adjustment disorder|1=Adjustment disorder}} in 2004, which forced her to withdraw from public life periodically. As crown princess and empress, Masako has accompanied her husband on official visits to foreign countries and at ceremonies within the imperial court. ==Early life and education== {{nihongo|Masako Owada|小和田雅子|''Owada Masako''}} was born on 9 December 1963 at Toranomon Hospital in [[:en:Toranomon, Minato, Tokyo|Toranomon]], {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|qid=Q190088|lt=Minato, Tokyo|1=Minato, Tokyo}}.Hills, p. 40. She is the eldest daughter of Yumiko Egashira (b. 1938) and {{ill|Hisashi Owada|en|Hisashi Owada|qid=Q701990|lt=Hisashi Owada|1=Hisashi Owada}} (b. 1932), a senior {{ill|Diplomat|en|Diplomat|qid=Q193391|lt=Diplomat|1=Diplomat}} and former president of the {{ill|International Court of Justice|en|International Court of Justice|qid=Q7801|lt=International Court of Justice|1=International Court of Justice}}. She has two younger sisters, [[:en:twins|twins]] named Setsuko and Reiko (b. 1966).{{cite journal|last1=Sanz|first1=Cynthia|url=http://www.people.com/people/archive/article/0,,20110658,00.html|title=The Princess Bride|journal=[[People (magazine)|People]]|date=21 June 1993|volume=39|issue=24|access-date=16 November 2011|archive-date=10 May 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120510175809/http://www.people.com/people/archive/article/0,,20110658,00.html|url-status=dead}} Masako went to live in {{ill|Moscow|en|Moscow|qid=Q649|lt=Moscow|1=Moscow}} with her parents when she was two years old, where she attended Detskiy Sad (kindergarten in Russian) No. 1127 daycare.Hills, pp. 42–44. At the age of five, Masako's family moved to {{ill|New York City|en|New York City|qid=Q60|lt=New York City|1=New York City}}, where she attended kindergarten at Public School 81 in Riverdale.{{Cite news |first=Zak |last=Kostro |url=https://www.riverdalepress.com/stories/their-former-classmate-would-become-japans-next-empress,68768 |title=Their former classmate would become Japan's next empress |date=April 28, 2019 |newspaper=The Riverdale Press}}Hills, p. 45. In 1971, the Owadas returned to Japan, moving in with Masako's maternal grandparents in {{ill|Meguro|en|Meguro|qid=Q233903|lt=Meguro|1=Meguro}} while Hisashi returned to the Foreign Ministry office.Hills, p. 46. She entered Futaba Gakuen, a private Roman Catholic girls' school in {{ill|Den-en-chōfu|en|Den-en-chōfu|qid=Q2613265|lt=Den-en-chōfu|1=Den-en-chōfu}}, Tokyo.Hills, p. 48. Established by the {{ill|Congregation of the Holy Infant Jesus|en|Congregation of the Holy Infant Jesus|qid=Q5160695|lt=Congregation of the Holy Infant Jesus|1=Congregation of the Holy Infant Jesus}} in 1872, Masako's mother and maternal grandmother had graduated from this school as well. It was here that Masako learned to play piano and tennis, joined a handicrafts club, and became interested in animals, tending several after school and deciding to become a {{ill|veterinarian|en|veterinarian|qid=Q202883|lt=veterinarian|1=Veterinarian}}.Hills, p. 49. Masako also studied her fourth and fifth languages, {{ill|French language|en|French language|qid=Q150|lt=French|1=French language}} and {{ill|German language|en|German language|qid=Q188|lt=German|1=German language}}.Hills, p. 50. With a school friend, Masako revived Futaba's softball team, serving as {{ill|third base|en|third base|qid=Q6859917|lt=third base|1=Third base}} and after three years bringing her team to the district championships.Hills, p. 52. In 1979, her second year of [[:en:senior high school|senior high school]], Masako and her family moved to the {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}} and settled in the {{ill|Boston|en|Boston|qid=Q100|lt=Boston|1=Boston}} suburb of {{ill|Belmont, Massachusetts|en|Belmont, Massachusetts|qid=Q816096|lt=Belmont, Massachusetts|1=Belmont, Massachusetts}}, where her father became a guest professor of international law at {{ill|Harvard University|en|Harvard University|qid=Q13371|lt=Harvard University|1=Harvard University}}'s {{ill|Weatherhead Center for International Affairs|en|Weatherhead Center for International Affairs|qid=Q7978292|lt=Center for International Affairs|1=Weatherhead Center for International Affairs}}.Hills, p. 92. In 1981, she graduated from [[:en:Belmont High School (Belmont, Massachusetts)|Belmont High School]], where she was president of the {{ill|National Honor Society|en|National Honor Society|qid=Q10335734|lt=National Honor Society|1=National Honor Society}}{{cite journal|last1=Green|first1=Michelle|date=25 January 1993|title=Princess Bride: Oft Rejected, Japan's Crown Prince Gets a 'Yes' from a Harvard Grad|journal=[[People (magazine)|People]]|volume=39|issue=3 |url=http://www.people.com/people/archive/article/0,,20109655,00.html |access-date=16 November 2011}} and participated in the school's math team and French club. Masako joined the school's softball team and won a [[:en:Goethe Society|Goethe Society]] award for her [[:en:German poetry|German poetry]]. Masako participated in a production of ''{{ill|M*A*S*H|en|M*A*S*H|qid=Q2471116|lt=M*A*S*H|1=M*A*S*H}}''. Masako's father was posted to {{ill|Moscow|en|Moscow|qid=Q649|lt=Moscow|1=Moscow}} after her high school graduation, but Masako remained in Boston to continue her education;Hills, p. 95. In 1981 she enrolled at {{ill|Harvard College|en|Harvard College|qid=Q49123|lt=Harvard College|1=Harvard College}}, where she chaired the school's Japan Society, "became quite close friends with the then Japanese consul in Boston, and volunteered as a kind of self-appointed diplomat and cultural ambassador"Hills, p. 101. in the wake of mounting {{ill|Japan–United States relations|en|Japan–United States relations|qid=Q1227243|lt=Japan–United States|1=Japan–United States relations}} trade tension. Masako liked to ski and traveled overseas during vacations, staying with a host family in {{ill|France|en|France|qid=Q142|lt=France|1=France}} and studying at the {{ill|Goethe-Institut|en|Goethe-Institut|qid=Q157033|lt=Goethe-Institut|1=Goethe-Institut}}. Masako is fluent in English and in French, which she learned in 1983 at the University Center for French Studies at [[:en:Université Grenoble Alpes|Université Grenoble Alpes]].{{Cite web|url=https://cuef.univ-grenoble-alpes.fr/menu-principal/presentation/alumni/masako-owada-190493.kjsp?RH=1490364328441|title=Masako Owada|first=Anne-Claire|last=Flochlay|website=Centre universitaire d'études françaises - Université Grenoble Alpes }} Masako worked with {{ill|Jeffrey Sachs|en|Jeffrey Sachs|qid=Q319189|lt=Jeffrey Sachs|1=Jeffrey Sachs}} to obtain a {{ill|Bachelor of Arts|en|Bachelor of Arts|qid=Q1765120|lt=B.A.|1=Bachelor of Arts}} ''[[:en:magna cum laude|magna cum laude]]'' in {{ill|Economics|en|Economics|qid=Q8134|lt=Economics|1=Economics}} in March 1985.Hills, pp. 106–107. ==Professional career== [[File:Oxford - Balliol College - geograph.org.uk - 1329613.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Balliol College, Oxford|en|Balliol College, Oxford|qid=Q805285|lt=Balliol College|1=Balliol College, Oxford}}, {{ill|University of Oxford|en|University of Oxford|qid=Q34433|lt=Oxford|1=University of Oxford}}, where Masako studied {{ill|International relations|en|International relations|qid=Q166542|lt=International relations|1=International relations}}]] After graduation Masako moved back to Japan, where for six months (April to October 1986) she studied law at the {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=University of Tokyo|1=University of Tokyo}} to prepare for the {{ill|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|en|Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|qid=Q222241|lt=Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs|1=Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)}}'s entrance examination.{{cite news|title=Princess Masako: Weight of Imperial world on Princess Masako|last1=Sato|first1=Mariko|url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20090519i1.html|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|date=19 May 2009|access-date=16 November 2011}}Hills, p. 115. Out of 800 applicants only 28 passed; Masako was one of them, along with two other women.{{cite web|url=http://www.biography.com/people/princess-masako-9542219|title=Princess Masako Biography|publisher=Biography.com|access-date=22 October 2016}} "She was assigned, first, to the oddly named Second International Organizations Division which deals with Japan's relations with international agencies, such as the {{ill|OECD|en|OECD|qid=Q41550|lt=OECD|1=OECD}}, a club of 30 rich countries committed to free trade and development. Her assignments included dealing with the OECD's environmental affairs committee ... by all accounts she acquitted herself well—her command of spoken languages, so rare in Japan, was a huge advantage—and was popular with most of her workmates."Hills, p. 116. During her free time, Masako attended cooking classes to, according to interviews with her instructor, "be able to cook proper Japanese dishes when she was entertaining [foreigners]."Hills, p. 119. Two years later, in 1988, Masako was chosen by the Ministry to be sponsored for two years' postgraduate study overseas with full pay, just as her father Hisashi had been years earlier.Hills, pp. 137, 155. Masako "desperately wanted to go back to Harvard to do her master's". According to her former Harvard adviser Oliver Oldman, she "tried to re-enroll to work towards ... a Juris Doctor. However, Harvard's bureaucrats would not give her credit for her study-time at the University of Tokyo."Hills, pp. 152–153. Therefore, Masako enrolled in her second choice, studying {{ill|International relations|en|International relations|qid=Q166542|lt=International relations|1=International relations}} under [[:en:Sir Adam Roberts|Sir Adam Roberts]]Hills, p. 139. at {{ill|Balliol College, Oxford|en|Balliol College, Oxford|qid=Q805285|lt=Balliol College|1=Balliol College, Oxford}}, {{ill|University of Oxford|en|University of Oxford|qid=Q34433|lt=Oxford|1=University of Oxford}}.Hills, p. 155. However, for unclear reasons Masako did not finish her thesisHills, pp. 140–141. and instead returned to Japan in 1990. ==Courtship and marriage== {{multiple image | align = right |perrow = 1/1 | total_width = 250 | image1 = Emperor Naruhito and Empress Masako in formal wedding robes.jpg | caption1 = The newly married Crown Prince Naruhito and Crown Princess Masako in Japanese traditional attire, with the Prince wearing a ''[[sokutai]]'', the Princess a ''{{ill|Jūnihitoe|en|Jūnihitoe|qid=Q2418740|lt=Jūnihitoe|1=Jūnihitoe}}'', 1993 | image2 = Japanese 500-yen commemorative coin 1993 Imperial Wedding.jpg | caption2 = A 500-yen coin issued to commemorate the imperial wedding | image3 = Rosa rugosa 2010.jpg | caption3 = Crimson phenotype of Japanese 'Ramanas' rose, ''{{ill|Rosa rugosa|en|Rosa rugosa|qid=Q634975|lt=Rosa rugosa|1=Rosa rugosa}}'', Masako's designated imperial personal insignia }} Masako first met {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Prince Naruhito|1=Naruhito}} at a tea for {{ill|Infanta Elena, Duchess of Lugo|en|Infanta Elena, Duchess of Lugo|qid=Q54386|lt=Infanta Elena of Spain|1=Infanta Elena, Duchess of Lugo}}, in November 1986,{{cite web|url=http://www.aol.com/style/|title=AOL Style News, Trends and Advice|last=AOL}}Hills, p .120. during her studies at the {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=University of Tokyo|1=University of Tokyo}}. The prince was immediately captivated by herHills, pp. 120–121. and arranged for them to meet several times over the next few weeks.Hills, p. 123. Because of this they were pursued relentlessly by the press throughout 1987.Hills, p. 136. However, Masako's name disappeared from the list of possible royal brides due to controversy about her maternal grandfather, Yutaka Egashira, who while working for the {{ill|Industrial Bank of Japan|en|Industrial Bank of Japan|qid=Q4119349|lt=Industrial Bank of Japan|1=Industrial Bank of Japan}} was assigned to take over management of one of its creditors the {{ill|Chisso|en|Chisso|qid=Q3487925|lt=Chisso|1=Chisso}} Corporation to prevent it from financial collapse.Hills, p. 133. Chisso, built in the 1930s, dumped the {{ill|methylmercury|en|methylmercury|qid=Q414075|lt=methylmercury|1=Methylmercury}} used to make {{ill|acetaldehyde|en|acetaldehyde|qid=Q61457|lt=acetaldehyde|1=Acetaldehyde}} (a chemical in plastic) into the water surrounding [[:en:Minamata|Minamata]] and other towns, causing the infamous {{ill|Minamata disease|en|Minamata disease|qid=Q338857|lt=Minamata disease|1=Minamata disease}} and the resulting scandal. Despite this controversy and Masako's travelling to [[:en:Oxford University|Oxford University]]'s [[:en:Balliol College|Balliol College]] for the next two years, Naruhito remained interested in her. Masako refused to marry the prince because it would force her to give up her promising career in diplomacy and severely restrict her independence and freedoms. Masako finally accepted his third proposal on 9 December 1992. It was reported that he argued that serving as Crown Princess of Japan would only be "another form of diplomacy" before she finally accepted this third proposal.{{cite journal|last1=Green|first1=Michelle|date=25 January 1993|title=Princess Bride: Oft Rejected, Japan's Crown Prince Gets a 'Yes' from a Harvard Grad|journal=[[People (magazine)|People]]|volume=39|issue=3 |url=http://www.people.com/people/archive/article/0,,20109655,00.html|access-date=9 August 2013}} The {{ill|Imperial Household Council|en|Imperial Household Council|qid=Q338120|lt=Imperial Household Council|1=Imperial Household Council}} formally announced the engagement on 19 January 1993; the engagement ceremony was held on 12 April 1993. Although many were surprised at the news, as it was believed that the prince and Masako had separated, the engagement was met with a surge of renewed media attention directed toward the Imperial family and their new princess. Masako married Crown Prince Naruhito in a traditional wedding ceremony on 9 June 1993.Hills, p. 2. By virtue of the marriage, Masako Owada assumed the formal style ''Her Imperial Highness The Crown Princess of Japan''. As tradition dictates, upon her entry into the imperial family and like other members, she received the blossom of the endemic curly-leaved Japanese Ramanas rose ({{nihongo|''o-shirushi''|お印}}): ''{{ill|Rosa rugosa|en|Rosa rugosa|qid=Q634975|lt=Rosa rugosa|1=Rosa rugosa}}'' ({{nihongo|''hamanasu'', beach aubergine|ハマナス}}){{cite web |title=Harmonia Appendix A: Rosa rugosa |url=http://projekty.gdos.gov.pl/files/artykuly/127091/Rosa-rugosa_roza-pomarszczona_EN_icon.pdf |website=Projektny Generalnej Dyreckji Ochrony Srodowiska |publisher=Uniwersytet Slaski w Kotwicach |access-date=20 January 2023}}{{cite web |title=Agriculture |url=https://www.essentia-allone.com/en/agriculture.html |website=Essentia |publisher=Essentia Limited Company, Hokkaido, Japan |access-date=22 January 2023}} for an imperial personal {{ill|Emblem|en|Emblem|qid=Q112110|lt=Emblem|1=Emblem}}, which is mauve-lilac to crimson or white (rarely with cultivars to full or burst yellow),{{cite web |title=The Plant Guide: 'Agnes' rugosa rose |url=https://www.finegardening.com/plant/agnes-rugosa-rose-rosa-rugosa-agnes |website=FineGardening.com |publisher=Taunton |access-date=24 January 2023}} while her insignia as Crown Princess bear the forms of the imperial household's antique gold-traced white chrysanthemum.{{cite book |last1=McClatchie |first1=Thomas R. H. |title=Japanese Heraldry |date=2010 |publisher=Kessinger Publishing, LLC |location=Whitefish |isbn=978-1162937397 |pages=6–13, ff}} In addition, she was placed in the Japanese Imperial Order of Precedence (used for the most formal occasions) behind her mother-in-law, {{ill|Empress Michiko|en|Empress Michiko|qid=Q87610|lt=Empress Michiko|1=Empress Michiko}}, and her grandmother-in-law, {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|qid=Q230697|lt=Empress Dowager Nagako|1=Empress Nagako}}. The {{ill|orchid|en|orchid|qid=Q25308|lt=orchid|1=Orchid}} flower ''{{ill|Dendrobium|en|Dendrobium|qid=Q133778|lt=Dendrobium|1=Dendrobium}}'' cultivar ''Masako Kotaishi Hidenka'' was named in her honour to celebrate the wedding.{{cite web|url=http://www.pbase.com/image/19286164|title=Orchid 5. Dendrobium Masako Kotaishi Hidenka|website=PBase.com|access-date=21 February 2018}} Masako became the third commoner to marry into the imperial family, after her mother-in-law, {{ill|Empress Michiko|en|Empress Michiko|qid=Q87610|lt=Empress Emerita Michiko|1=Empress Michiko}} (Michiko Shōda) and her sister-in-law, {{ill|Kiko, Crown Princess of Japan|en|Kiko, Crown Princess of Japan|qid=Q232636|lt=Crown Princess Kiko|1=Kiko, Crown Princess of Japan}} (Kiko Kawashima). ===Children=== [[File:Naruhito, Masako and Aiko in Tochigi Prefecture.jpg|thumb|Naruhito and Masako with their daughter, Aiko, in {{ill|Nasu Imperial Villa|en|Nasu Imperial Villa|qid=Q4012794|lt=Nasu Imperial Villa|1=Nasu Imperial Villa}}, {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|qid=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}} in August 2019]] Masako's first pregnancy was announced in December 1999, but she {{ill|Miscarriage|en|Miscarriage|qid=Q28693|lt=miscarried|1=Miscarriage}}.{{cite news |title=Royal life takes its toll on Japan's crown princess|url=http://www.chinadaily.com.cn/english/doc/2004-08/02/content_356946.htm|newspaper=[[China Daily]]|date=2 August 2004 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110606231921/http://www.chinadaily.com.cn/english/doc/2004-08/02/content_356946.htm |archive-date=6 June 2011|access-date=16 November 2011}} The Emperor and Empress have one daughter: {{nihongo|{{ill|Aiko, Princess Toshi|en|Aiko, Princess Toshi|qid=Q743509|lt=Aiko, Princess Toshi|1=Aiko, Princess Toshi}}|敬宮愛子内親王|Toshi-no-miya Aiko Naishinnō|extra=born 1 December 2001 at Imperial Household Agency Hospital in [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]]}}.{{cite news|title=Girl Born to Japan's Princess|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2001/12/01/world/girl-born-to-japan-s-princess.html|newspaper={{ill|The New York Times|en|The New York Times|qid=Q9684|lt=The New York Times|1=The New York Times}} |date=1 December 2001|access-date=16 November 2011}}{{cite news|title=Japan: A Name For The Royal Baby|last1=French |first1=Howard W.|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2001/12/08/world/world-briefing-asia-japan-a-name-for-the-royal-baby.html|newspaper={{ill|The New York Times|en|The New York Times|qid=Q9684|lt=The New York Times|1=The New York Times}}|date=8 December 2001|access-date=16 November 2011}} ===Succession crisis=== Their daughter's birth, which occurred more than eight years after their marriage, sparked {{ill|Japanese imperial succession debate|en|Japanese imperial succession debate|qid=Q2996090|lt=lively debate|1=Japanese imperial succession debate}} in Japan about whether ''{{ill|Imperial Household Law|en|Imperial Household Law|qid=Q1324733|lt=The Imperial Household Law of 1947|1=Imperial Household Law}}'' should be changed from that of [[:en:Agnatic primogeniture|Agnatic primogeniture]] to either cognatic or absolute primogeniture, which would allow a woman to succeed to the {{ill|Chrysanthemum Throne|en|Chrysanthemum Throne|qid=Q1981690|lt=Chrysanthemum Throne|1=Chrysanthemum Throne}}. A government-appointed panel of experts submitted a report on 25 October 2005, recommending that the Imperial Succession Law be amended to permit absolute primogeniture. On 20 January 2006, Prime Minister {{ill|Junichiro Koizumi|en|Junichiro Koizumi|qid=Q130852|lt=Junichiro Koizumi|1=Junichiro Koizumi}} used part of his annual keynote speech to address the controversy when he pledged to submit a bill to the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}} letting women ascend to the throne in order that the imperial throne be continued in a stable manner. Koizumi did not announce a timing for the legislation to be introduced nor did he provide details about the content, but he did note that it would be in line with the conclusions of the 2005 Government Panel. Plans to change the male-only law of Imperial succession were shelved after it was announced in February 2006 that Masako's brother-in-law and his wife, [[:en:Fumihito, Prince Akishino|Prince]] and [[:en:Kiko, Princess Akishino|Princess Akishino]], were expecting their third child. On 6 September 2006, Princess Akishino gave birth to a son, {{ill|Prince Hisahito of Akishino|en|Prince Hisahito of Akishino|qid=Q844333|lt=Hisahito|1=Prince Hisahito of Akishino}}, who was third-in-line to the Chrysanthemum Throne under the current law, after his uncle, the Emperor, and his father, Prince Akishino.{{cite news|title=Japan princess gives birth to boy|work={{ill|BBC News|en|BBC News|qid=Q1160945|lt=BBC News|1=BBC News}}|date=6 September 2006|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/5316266.stm|access-date=6 September 2006}}{{cite magazine|last1=Walsh|first1=Bryan|date=5 September 2006|title=Japan Celebrates: It's a Boy!|magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |url=http://www.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1531895,00.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070513055227/http://www.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1531895,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=May 13, 2007|access-date=16 November 2011}}.{{cite news|title= Japan's Imperial Family: Life in the Cloudy Imperial Fishbowl|last1=Yoshida|first1=Reiji |url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20070327i1.html|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|date=27 March 2007|agency=FYI (weekly column)|access-date=16 November 2011}} ==Crown Princess of Japan== [[File:Princess Masako, the present Empress of Japan.jpg|thumb|left|Masako, as Crown Princess]] In 1994, the couple visited {{ill|Saudi Arabia|en|Saudi Arabia|qid=Q851|lt=Saudi Arabia|1=Saudi Arabia}}, {{ill|Oman|en|Oman|qid=Q842|lt=Oman|1=Oman}}, {{ill|Qatar|en|Qatar|qid=Q846|lt=Qatar|1=Qatar}} and {{ill|Bahrain|en|Bahrain|qid=Q398|lt=Bahrain|1=Bahrain}}. They visited {{ill|Kuwait|en|Kuwait|qid=Q817|lt=Kuwait|1=Kuwait}}, the {{ill|United Arab Emirates|en|United Arab Emirates|qid=Q878|lt=United Arab Emirates|1=United Arab Emirates}} and {{ill|Jordan|en|Jordan|qid=Q810|lt=Jordan|1=Jordan}} in 1995, and again traveled to Jordan in 1999.{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/activity/activity02-before.html|title=Their Imperial Highnesses Crown Prince Naruhito and Crown Princess Masako|publisher=kunaicho.go.jp|access-date=23 October 2012}} In 1999, they also went to {{ill|Belgium|en|Belgium|qid=Q31|lt=Belgium|1=Belgium}} to attend the wedding of [[:en:Prince Philippe, Duke of Brabant|Prince Philippe, Duke of Brabant]]. In 2002, they paid a visit to {{ill|New Zealand|en|New Zealand|qid=Q664|lt=New Zealand|1=New Zealand}} and {{ill|Australia|en|Australia|qid=Q408|lt=Australia|1=Australia}}. In 2006, the Crown Prince and Crown Princess went to the {{ill|Netherlands|en|Netherlands|qid=Q55|lt=Netherlands|1=Netherlands}} with their daughter, Princess Aiko, at the invitation of {{ill|Beatrix of the Netherlands|en|Beatrix of the Netherlands|qid=Q29574|lt=Queen Beatrix|1=Beatrix of the Netherlands}} for a private visit.{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/shinzen/gaikoku/gaikoku-1999-2008.html|title=List of Overseas Visits by the Emperor, Empress and Imperial Family (1999 – 2008)|publisher=The Imperial Household Agency|access-date=29 December 2012}} [[File:Crown Prince Naruhito and Crown Princess Masako 20161107 1.jpg|thumb|right|Crown Prince Naruhito and Crown Princess Masako attended the {{ill|JET Programme|en|JET Programme|qid=Q1059780|lt=JET Programme|1=JET Programme}} 30th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony at the {{ill|Keio Plaza Hotel|en|Keio Plaza Hotel|qid=Q6383787|lt=Keio Plaza Hotel|1=Keio Plaza Hotel}} in Tokyo, November 2016.]] On 30 April 2013, the Crown Prince and Crown Princess were present at the inauguration of King {{ill|Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands|en|Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands|qid=Q154952|lt=Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands|1=Willem-Alexander of the Netherlands}}, which was the Crown Princess's first official overseas appearance in eleven years.{{cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2013/05/01/national/japans-crown-prince-princess-attend-coronation-of-new-dutch-king/#.UYqeQ6PGfIU|title=Japan's Crown Prince and Princess attend the coronation of the new Dutch king|date=May 2013|publisher=japantimes.co.jp|access-date=8 May 2013}}{{cite web|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2013/04/29/national/crown-prince-princess-depart-for-netherlands/#.UYqeS6PGfIU|title=The Crown Prince and Princess depart for Netherlands|date=29 April 2013|publisher=japantimes.co.jp |access-date=8 May 2013}}{{cite web|url=http://www.japantoday.com/Category:11{{subst:NAMESPACE}}]] }} }} }} [[Category/national/view/crown-prince-princess-return-from-netherlands|title=The Crown Prince and Princess return from Netherlands|date=3 May 2013 |publisher=japantoday.com|access-date=8 May 2013}} In October 2014, she was present at a banquet held in honour of King Willem-Alexander and {{ill|Queen Máxima of the Netherlands|en|Queen Máxima of the Netherlands|qid=Q460960|lt=Queen Máxima|1=Queen Máxima of the Netherlands}} at the Tokyo Imperial Palace, which was her first appearance in such a ceremony in eleven years.{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-29828756|title=Japan Crown Princess Masako attends first banquet in 11 years|work=BBC News|date=30 October 2014|access-date=13 May 2015}} She welcomed the couple during an official ceremony at the palace which was her first appearance in a welcoming ceremony after five years. In July 2015, Princess Masako traveled to {{ill|Tonga|en|Tonga|qid=Q678|lt=Tonga|1=Tonga}} with the Crown Prince in order to attend the Coronation of King {{ill|Tupou VI|en|Tupou VI|qid=Q208497|lt=Tupou VI|1=Tupou VI}}. Over 40 members of the Japanese media covered the event, during which the happy-looking Crown Princess was warmly welcomed.{{cite news|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/07/06/national/crown-princess-masako-unexpectedly-joins-meeting-with-japanese-in-tonga/#.WALkY_l97IU|title=Crown Princess Masako unexpectedly joins meeting with Japanese in Tonga|publisher=The Japan Times|date=6 July 2015|access-date=15 October 2016}} ==Empress of Japan== {{see also|2019 Japanese imperial transition}} Upon the abdication of Masako's father-in-law, Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|qid=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}} of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei era|1=Heisei era}} on 30 April 2019, his eldest son, Crown Prince Naruhito became the 126th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, and his wife, Masako became {{ill|Empress consort|en|Empress consort|qid=Q7723211|lt=Empress consort|1=Empress consort}} on 1 May. The Emperor and Empress of {{ill|Reiwa era|en|Reiwa era|qid=Q62609017|lt=Reiwa era|1=Reiwa era}} were [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthroned]] (''Sokui Rei Seiden no Gi'') at the [[:en:Imperial Palace, Tokyo|Tokyo Imperial Palace]] on 22 October 2019. Naruhito and Masako's first trip abroad as emperor and empress took place in September 2022, to the {{ill|United Kingdom|en|United Kingdom|qid=Q145|lt=United Kingdom|1=United Kingdom}} to attend the {{ill|Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II|en|Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II|qid=Q113846055|lt=state funeral|1=Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II}} of Queen {{ill|Elizabeth II|en|Elizabeth II|qid=Q9682|lt=Elizabeth II|1=Elizabeth II}}.{{cite news| url=https://www.bbc.com/news/in-pictures-62918196| title=The Queen's funeral in pictures| work=BBC News| date=19 September 2022}}{{cite news| date=2022-09-10| title=Emperor Naruhito plans to attend Queen Elizabeth's funeral in first overseas trip| url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2022/09/10/national/emperor-naruhito-queen-funeral/| access-date=2022-09-11| website=The Japan Times| location=Tokyo| agency={{ill|Kyodo News|en|Kyodo News|qid=Q779730|lt=Kyodo News|1=Kyodo News}}, Reuters| language=en-US}} They visited {{ill|Indonesia|en|Indonesia|qid=Q252|lt=Indonesia|1=Indonesia}} in June 2023, their first state visit.{{cite news |title=Japan's emperor in Indonesia for first state visit |url=https://www.thejakartapost.com/amp/world/2023/06/19/japans-emperor-in-indonesia-for-first-state-visit.html |access-date=23 June 2023 |work=The Jakarta Post |date=19 June 2023}}{{cite news |title=Indonesians welcome Japan's Emperor, Empress |url=https://www3.nhk.or.jp/nhkworld/en/news/videos/20230621212449354/ |access-date=6 September 2023 |work=[[NHK World-Japan]] |date=21 June 2023}} In March 2024, Masako and Naruhito visited {{ill|Wajima, Ishikawa|en|Wajima, Ishikawa|qid=Q823896|lt=Wajima|1=Wajima, Ishikawa}} and {{ill|Suzu, Ishikawa|en|Suzu, Ishikawa|qid=Q846345|lt=Suzu|1=Suzu, Ishikawa}}, two cities affected by the {{ill|2024 Noto earthquake|en|2024 Noto earthquake|qid=Q124060919|lt=Noto earthquake|1=2024 Noto earthquake}}. The couple later visited an evacuation center in {{ill|Anamizu, Ishikawa|en|Anamizu, Ishikawa|qid=Q1349353|lt=Anamizu|1=Anamizu, Ishikawa}} on 12 April.{{Cite web |title=Imperial couple make second visit to the stricken Noto Peninsula {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/15228848 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}} Masako and Naruhito embarked on a three-day state visit to the United Kingdom in late June 2024, at the invitation of [[:en:King Charles III|King Charles III]].{{Cite web |date=2024-06-04 |title=Japan's emperor and empress to pay three-day state visit to U.K. |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2024/06/04/japan/japan-emperor-uk-visit/ |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=The Japan Times}} The imperial couple had originally planned to visit in 2020 as guests of Queen Elizabeth II, but the state visit was cancelled due to the {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom|en|COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom|qid=Q84167106|lt=COVID-19 pandemic|1=COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom}}.{{Cite web |date=2024-06-05 |title=King Charles and Queen Camilla to Host Japan State Visit in June |url=https://www.townandcountrymag.com/society/tradition/a60618019/king-charles-japan-state-visit-june-2024-news/ |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=Town & Country}} The rescheduled visit went ahead despite concerns of postponement due to the {{ill|2024 United Kingdom general election|en|2024 United Kingdom general election|qid=Q78851988|lt=British general election|1=2024 United Kingdom general election}} campaign that began in late May.{{Cite web |date=2024-06-04 |title=Prince William to Take on Key Role in Japanese State Visit to the UK |url=https://uk.news.yahoo.com/prince-william-key-role-japanese-022100717.html |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=Yahoo News}} It was the first state visit in modern times to take place during an active election campaign.{{Cite web |title=Japanese state visit to UK not stopped by general election |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cxww0e2xlnxo |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=www.bbc.com}} ==Health== Masako has periodically been out of the public eye, largely between 2004 and 2014,{{cite news|title=Japan's Princess Masako opens up on insecurities and health|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-46498754|publisher=BBC|date=9 Dec 2018}} reportedly due to emotional disorders speculated to be caused by the pressure to produce a male heir and adjusting to life in the Imperial Family.{{cite news |url=https://www.google.com/hostednews/afp/article/ALeqM5iKOMfNLRjO_gBnrVAhIzh7dtyXLw|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090306224417/http://www.google.com/hostednews/afp/article/ALeqM5iKOMfNLRjO_gBnrVAhIzh7dtyXLw|url-status=dead|archive-date=6 March 2009|title=Japan princess makes rare solo public visit |agency=[[Agence France-Presse]]|date=2 March 2009|access-date=16 November 2011}}{{cite news |title=Tabloids turn against the Crown Princess Masako|last1=Lewis|first1=Leo |url=http://www.timesonline.co.uk/tol/news/world/asia/article3307711.ece?print=yes&randnum=1151003209000|newspaper={{ill|The Times|en|The Times|qid=Q50008|lt=The Times|1=The Times}}|location=London|date=5 February 2008|access-date=16 November 2011}}{{dead link|date=September 2024|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} In July 2004, she was diagnosed as suffering from {{ill|adjustment disorder|en|adjustment disorder|qid=Q317158|lt=adjustment disorder|1=Adjustment disorder}} and was reported to be seeking treatment.{{cite news|title=Royal wives seek new role in monarchies|url=http://www.chinadaily.com.cn/english/doc/2004-07/13/content_347829.htm|newspaper=[[China Daily]]|date=13 July 2004 |access-date=16 November 2011}}{{cite news |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/asia-pacific/3939179.stm|title=Princess trapped by palace guard|date=3 February 2006|publisher=BBC|access-date=16 November 2011}}{{cite news|title='Imperial diplomacy' proves elusive dream|last1=Sakamoto|first1=Mie|url=http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20080614f2.html|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}} |date=14 June 2008|agency={{ill|Kyodo News|en|Kyodo News|qid=Q779730|lt=Kyodo News|1=Kyodo News}}|access-date=16 November 2011}} On 11 July 2008, Naruhito sought public understanding for his ailing wife. He was on an eight-day trip to {{ill|Spain|en|Spain|qid=Q29|lt=Spain|1=Spain}} without her:{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/asia/the-sadness-behind-the-chrysanthemum-throne-6169426.html|title=The sadness behind the Chrysanthemum Throne|location=London|work=The Independent|date=22 May 2004}} "I would like the public to understand that Masako is continuing to make her utmost efforts with the help of those around her. Please continue to watch over her kindly and over the long term." Pressures to produce a male heir, to conform with the ancient traditions and a {{ill|Imperial Household Law|en|Imperial Household Law|qid=Q1324733|lt=1947 Imperial Household Law|1=Imperial Household Law}} are perceived to be behind her illness, as well as negative media coverage of her behavior, the stress of royal responsibility and public life, and turf battles among the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}.{{cite news|url=http://www.gmanews.tv/story/106446/Japans-crown-prince-seeks-public-understanding-for-ailing-princess|title=Japan's crown prince seeks public understanding for ailing princess|agency={{ill|Associated Press|en|Associated Press|qid=Q40469|lt=Associated Press|1=Associated Press}}|date=11 July 2008|publisher=[[GMA News and Public Affairs]]|access-date=16 November 2011|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110522062300/http://www.gmanews.tv/story/106446/Japans-crown-prince-seeks-public-understanding-for-ailing-princess|archive-date=22 May 2011}}Schreiber, Mark, "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2012/01/01/national/media-national/japans-troubled-royals-put-up-a-brave-front/ Japan's troubled royals put up a brave front]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'' 1 January 2012, p. 13. In December 2012, at the time of her 49th birthday, Masako issued a statement thanking the Japanese people for their support and saying that she was still receiving treatment for her illness.{{cite news|title=Japan's Crown Princess Masako discusses her illness on 49th birthday|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/asia/japan/9734410/Japans-Crown-Princess-Masako-discusses-her-illness-on-49th-birthday.html|newspaper=Daily Telegraph|date=10 Dec 2012}} The Japanese Constitution does not allow the members of the Imperial Family to engage in political activities. Naruhito made controversial comments about discourtesies and pressures placed on his wife by the Imperial Household Agency and his wife's desire to pursue the life of a diplomat.{{cite news|title=Airing wife's troubles a turning point: prince|last1=Cameron|first1=Deborah |url=http://www.theage.com.au/news/World/Airing-wifes-troubles-a-turning-point-prince/2005/02/23/1109046986391.html|newspaper=[[The Age]] |date=24 February 2005|location=Melbourne|access-date=16 November 2011}} In 2019, Masako accompanied her husband at official events and at his accession ceremonies. During the state visit of U.S. President {{ill|Donald Trump|en|Donald Trump|qid=Q22686|lt=Donald Trump|1=Donald Trump}} and First Lady {{ill|Melania Trump|en|Melania Trump|qid=Q432473|lt=Melania Trump|1=Melania Trump}} to Japan in April, Masako and Naruhito conversed with them without interpreters; the imperial couple are both fluent in English. Her doctors stated that she has not fully recovered but a strong sense of duty has helped her fulfill her responsibilities.{{cite news | last = Yamaguchi | first = Mari | date = 8 December 2019 | title = Japan Empress Turns 56, Still Recovering Her Mental Health | url = https://www.usnews.com/news/world/articles/2019-12-08/japan-empress-turns-56-still-recovering-her-mental-health | work = U.S. News & World Report | location = New York, New York | access-date = 26 November 2020 }} ==Titles, styles and honours== ===Titles and styles=== *9 June 1993 – 30 April 2019: ''Her Imperial Highness'' The Crown Princess{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/history/history02.html|title=Their Imperial Highnesses the Crown Prince and Crown Princess - The Imperial Household Agency|website=www.kunaicho.go.jp|access-date=12 July 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181231062706/http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/history/history02.html|archive-date=31 December 2018}} (皇太子妃殿下 ''Kōtaishi-hi Denka'') *1 May 2019 – present: ''Her Majesty'' The Empress{{cite web|url=http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/history/history02.html|title=Their Majesties the Emperor and Empress - The Imperial Household Agency|website=www.kunaicho.go.jp|access-date=1 May 2019}} (皇后陛下 ''Kōgō Heika'') ===Honours=== {{see also|List of honours of the Japanese imperial family by country}} ====Foreign==== * {{flag|Austria}}: {{ill|Decoration of Honour for Services to the Republic of Austria|en|Decoration of Honour for Services to the Republic of Austria|qid=Q94044|lt=Grand Decoration of Honour in Gold with Sash for Services to the Republic of Austria|1=Decoration of Honour for Services to the Republic of Austria}} (1999){{cite web|url= http://www.parlament.gv.at/PAKT/VHG/XXIV/AB/AB_10542/imfname_251156.pdf|title=Reply to a parliamentary question about the Decoration of Honour|language=de|page=1299|access-date=28 November 2012}} * {{flag|Hungary}}: Grand Cross of the [[:en:Order of Merit of the Republic of Hungary|Order of Merit of the Republic of Hungary]] (2000){{cite web|url=https://magyarkozlony.hu/dokumentumok/71864f464d306a3d655dbff967cb35b6aa48fcd0/megtekintes|title=Hungarian Journal, State Gazette issue 64, 2000. june 23.|language=hu|page=3830}} * {{flag|Malaysia}}: Honorary Grand Commander of the {{ill|Order of the Defender of the Realm|en|Order of the Defender of the Realm|qid=Q2074734|lt=Order of the Defender of the Realm|1=Order of the Defender of the Realm}} (2012){{cite web|url=http://www.istiadat.gov.my/index.php/component/semakanlantikanskp/|title=Semakan Penerima Darjah Kebesaran, Bintang dan Pingat Persekutuan.|access-date=2016-06-15|archive-date=2019-07-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190719195551/http://www.istiadat.gov.my/index.php/component/semakanlantikanskp|url-status=dead}} * {{flag|Netherlands}}: Grand Cross of the {{ill|Order of the Crown (Netherlands)|en|Order of the Crown (Netherlands)|qid=Q2272104|lt=Order of the Crown|1=Order of the Crown (Netherlands)}} (29 October 2014)[http://www.koninklijkhuis.nl/nieuws/nieuwsberichten/2014/oktober/decoraties-staatsbezoeken-japan-en-republiek-korea/ Decoraties Staatsbezoeken Japan en Republiek Korea] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141104190003/https://www.koninklijkhuis.nl/nieuws/nieuwsberichten/2014/oktober/decoraties-staatsbezoeken-japan-en-republiek-korea/ |date= 4 November 2014}} – website of the Dutch Royal House{{cite web|url=http://www.asahicom.jp/articles/images/AS20141029005403_comm.jpg |format=JPG |title=Photographic image |website=asahicom.jp |access-date=2022-07-12}} * {{flag|Portugal}}: Grand Cross of the {{ill|Order of Prince Henry|en|Order of Prince Henry|qid=Q93488|lt=Order of Prince Henry|1=Order of Prince Henry}} (2 December 1992){{cite web|url=http://www.ordens.presidencia.pt/?idc=154|title=''Cidadãos Estrangeiros Agraciados com Ordens Portuguesas'' |publisher=presidencia|language=pt|access-date=13 June 2012}} * {{flag|Spain}}: Dame Grand Cross of the {{ill|Order of Isabella the Catholic|en|Order of Isabella the Catholic|qid=Q931502|lt=Royal Order of Isabella the Catholic|1=Order of Isabella the Catholic}} (8 November 2008){{Cite web|url=https://www.boe.es/boe/dias/2008/11/10/pdfs/A44705-44705.pdf |title=Real Decreto 1872/2008 |publisher=Boletín Oficial del Estado }} ===Honorary positions=== * Honorary President of the [[:en:Japanese Red Cross|Japanese Red Cross]] Society (formerly vice president as the Crown Princess){{cite web|url=https://english.kyodonews.net/news/2019/05/2ed79a426529-empress-masako-conducts-1st-official-duty-in-new-capacity.html|title=Empress Masako conducts 1st official duty in new capacity|publisher=Kyodo News|date=22 May 2019|access-date=7 December 2020}} ===Honorary degrees=== * {{ill|Doctor of Civil Law|en|Doctor of Civil Law|qid=Q5287501|lt=Doctor of Civil Law|1=Doctor of Civil Law}}, {{ill|University of Oxford|en|University of Oxford|qid=Q34433|lt=University of Oxford|1=University of Oxford}} (2024){{cite news |last1=Freedland |first1=Jacob |title=Japanese empress given honorary degree by Oxford University |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/naruhito-oxford-university-oxford-japanese-lord-b2570808.html |access-date=28 June 2024 |work=The Independent |date=28 June 2024}}{{cite news|date=28 June 2024|title=【速報】皇后さまに「名誉法学博士号」授与 英オックスフォード大学|work=Nippon News Network|url=https://news.ntv.co.jp/Category:11{{subst:NAMESPACE}}]] }} }} }} [[Category/society/8701eacce29140ffbca65915729cb98a}} ==Ancestry== Her father Hisashi is descended from the Owada {{ill|Clan|en|Clan|qid=Q211503|lt=Clan|1=Clan}}, whose head Shinroku—Masako's 4th-great-grandfatherHills, p. 33.—was called to {{ill|Murakami Domain|en|Murakami Domain|qid=Q849483|lt=Murakami|1=Murakami Domain}} in 1787 to serve the Naito clan that the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] shōgun had installed as the city's rulers 67 years earlier. After the fall of the shogunate, the Owadas participated in a salmon-fishing cooperative,Hills, p. 35. the proceeds of which provided schooling for many local children, including Hisashi and his father Takeo. Her mother Yumiko is descended from the Egashira {{ill|Clan|en|Clan|qid=Q211503|lt=Clan|1=Clan}} which served the [[:en:Saga clan|Saga clan]]s near {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|qid=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki|1=Nagasaki}} on the island of [[Kyushu]]. One of the Egashiras, Yasutaro, went on to command a battleship in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}. Yumiko's father was a wealthy banker who was at the time of her marriage the managing director of the {{ill|Industrial Bank of Japan|en|Industrial Bank of Japan|qid=Q4119349|lt=Industrial Bank of Japan|1=Industrial Bank of Japan}}.Hills, p. 39. ==See also== * [[Emperor of Japan#Succession|Emperor of Japan: Succession]] * {{ill|Japanese imperial succession debate|en|Japanese imperial succession debate|qid=Q2996090|lt=Japanese imperial succession debate|1=Japanese imperial succession debate}} * ''{{ill|Princess Masako: Prisoner of the Chrysanthemum Throne|en|Princess Masako: Prisoner of the Chrysanthemum Throne|qid=Q7244747|lt=Princess Masako: Prisoner of the Chrysanthemum Throne|1=Princess Masako: Prisoner of the Chrysanthemum Throne}}'' ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Sources== *{{cite book|title=Princess Masako: Prisoner of the Chrysanthemum Throne|last=Hills|first=Ben|year=2006 |publisher=Jeremy P. Tarcher/Penguin|location=London; New York|isbn=1-58542-568-0|oclc=76074219 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zyg5_SYNe6UC&q=german,+Russian+and+Spanish+masako&pg=PA110}} ==External links== < < <
